question: What does labor productivity generally measure?
correctAnswer: Output produced per unit of labor input.
incorrectAnswers: Total profit divided by total labor cost only
The number of meetings held per week
How much cash the company has at the end of the month
explanation: Labor productivity focuses on how efficiently labor turns into output.

question: What does multifactor productivity (MFP) aim to capture?
correctAnswer: Improvements in output that aren’t explained solely by increases in labor or capital input.
incorrectAnswers: Only changes in raw wages with no link to output
A measure that ignores all inputs by definition
The expected stock price growth next quarter
explanation: MFP is used to reflect how effectively the combination of inputs produces more output, including efficiency and technology effects.

question: In operations measurement, how is cycle time different from throughput?
correctAnswer: Cycle time is how long a unit takes end-to-end; throughput is how many units complete in a given period.
incorrectAnswers: Cycle time counts completed units per hour; throughput measures delays
Throughput is only about employee headcount; cycle time is about budget size
They mean exactly the same thing with different words
explanation: Cycle time describes duration for work items, while throughput describes volume completed over time.

question: What is the difference between efficiency and effectiveness in performance thinking?
correctAnswer: Efficiency is using the fewest resources to achieve an output; effectiveness is achieving the intended results.
incorrectAnswers: Efficiency is about doing the right thing; effectiveness is about using fewer people
They are the same metric under different names
Effectiveness always ignores outcomes and only cares about costs
explanation: A process can be efficient (low cost) but not effective if it doesn’t achieve the desired outcome.

question: Why do organizations often look for waste reduction when they want higher productivity?
correctAnswer: Waste consumes resources without adding value, so removing it can improve output per resource.
incorrectAnswers: Waste reduction is only about cutting staff, not process work
Waste never affects output because it happens outside the main workflow
Waste reduction makes quality worse so inspection is cheaper
explanation: Reducing non-value-added work and defects lowers resource use and frees capacity, which improves productivity.

question: Which is a process-change example that can improve productivity without automatically changing demand?
correctAnswer: Eliminating handoff delays by redesigning the workflow so work moves more smoothly.
incorrectAnswers: Increasing marketing spend without changing production flow
Raising prices without changing how work gets done internally
Waiting to see if competitors improve first
explanation: Productivity can improve when internal processes reduce delays and rework, even if demand stays the same.
